observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
record all valid book reconciling items per Bank Reconciliation Statements in order to reflect correct CIB - LCCA balance at year-end; and
ensure that verified and valid stale checks must be reverted to the Cash in Bank account in order to present an accurate cash balance in the books of the LGU.
We also recommended that the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant exert all effort to verify the unreconciled amount of ₱889,056.33 and to make the necessary adjustments.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor mandate each LGU Officials responsible in the accounting and reporting of the local road network of the LGU to strictly follow the procedures outlined in COA Circular No. 2015-008 and monitor compliance thereon.
We also recommended that:
The Municipal Engineer provide the Municipal Accountant and GSO with the complete description of road components of road projects.
The Municipal Accountant maintain updated Local Road Network Ledger Cards with complete data/description of each component of the local road network.
The GSO maintain complete Local Road Network Property Cards with all the necessary data and prepare the Report on the Local Road Network.
The Inventory Team to prepare and submit the Report on the Physical Count of the Road Network System in accordance with the format provided under COA Circular 2015-008.

We recommended that the Inventory Committee conduct complete Physical Count of PPE and to specify the necessary information as provided in the RPCPPE form.
We also recommended that the Inventory Committee Vice-Chairperson, Municipal Accountant and GSO reconcile their records and any adjustments/corrections should be made in the books and/or the property records to put the accounts in order.
Furthermore, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor, Municipal Accountant and Property Custodian evaluate the list of non-existing/missing PPEs prepared by the Inventory Team and continue with the disposition procedures for the one time cleansing of PPE account in accordance with COA Circular 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020.

We recommended for the Municipal Accountant to submit to the audit team, a written justification and reconciliation of the noted discrepancies and to make the necessary adjustments, if any, in order to reflect the correct balances of assets and expenses accounts.
We also recommended that the LGU submit to the audit team certification from the supplier stating quantity and amount balance of Fuel, Oil, and Lubricants still owned by the LGU but kept in the former’s fuel depot as of December 31, 2022 and every quarter thereafter, as support to the presented inventory balance in the quarterly financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
recognize Accounts Payable only upon delivery of goods/services by the supplier and duly accepted by the LGU;
recognize asset account upon beneficial ownership and control clearly rests with the Municipality; and
henceforth, the audit team requests the submission of a list of accounts payable as of yearend, along with the corresponding photocopies of supporting documents to verify the validity of recognizing the liability accounts in the books.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
through all department heads concerned, expedite the implementation of development projects;
require the Municipal Engineer to prepare the necessary program of works for the implementation of CY 2022 continuing 20% DF projects; and
Henceforth,
require the Municipal Engineer, Municipal Planning Development Coordinator and Sangguniang Bayan to ensure that 20% development projects should be well-panned and procurement and implementation ready prior to budget allocation and appropriation; and
require the BAC to monitor and ensure the implementation of projects as planned in the Annual Procurement Plan.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct all LGU officials and employees responsible in the accounting and reporting of the receipt and utilization of MDRRM Fund to strictly observe the guidelines outlined in COA Circular 2014-002 and LBC Nos 140, 140A and 141.
We also recommended that the balance of ₱138,000.00 unutilized cash donations be returned to the donor if specified in the agreement or remit to the BTr if no condition is stated.

We recommended the Human Resource Management Officer (HRMO) require the Job Order Workers to submit the detailed and quantified accomplishment reports based on their actual daily work performed to form part of the supporting papers of their wages.
Likewise, the Municipal Accountant should ensure that a Disbursement Voucher (DV) is completely supported with the required documents.
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor, in consultation with the Municipal Budget Officer adopt with the mandated daily minimum wage rates as prescribed by the Regional Tripartite Wages and Productivity Board per Wage Order No. RB VIII-22.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant, Municipal Engineer and the GSO to submit the Inventory of Local Roads to DILG immediately and every three years thereafter, without need for demand from the said Office.

We recommended that the BAC Secretariat submit to the Audit Team the perfected contracts and Purchase Orders together with the supporting documents, within five working days from their execution, in accordance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, Municipal Accountant, Municipal Health Officer, Bids and Awards Committee Chairman submit a written explanation/justification for the deficiencies mentioned above.
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct all personnel concerned to stop the withdrawal of fuel, oil and lubricants without perfected contracts in accordance with RA 9184, and to stop advance payment unless allowed by the Philippine Law.
Henceforth, we recommended that procurement of fuel, oil and lubricants be meticulously and judiciously planned by the Municipality to avoid unlawful expenses.
This audit observation is without prejudice to the issuance of a Notice of Suspension/Disallowance, if warranted.

We recommended that the Municipal Health Officer:
submit a written justification to the audit team why funds were not utilized for many years; and
promptly follow the guidelines provided by the Philippine Health Insurance Corporation to use the funds, thereby enabling beneficiaries to enjoy its benefits.

We recommended the Municipal Treasurer, Municipal Accountant and Municipal Budget Officer submit documents and computation to support the savings claimed in the amount of ₱25,143,274.11 based on the aforementioned guidelines;
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor to immediately order to stop the appropriation and disbursements until savings from unappropriated surplus is duly supported.

require the Municipal Engineer and Municipal Agriculturist to prepare the Inventory, Inspection and Appraisal Form and Report of Waste Material and submit to the Audit Team for validation.

require the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Engineer to re-compute the cost of the damaged properties based on the prescribed formula provided by the DBM as contained in the Manual on Disposal of Government Property; 

require the Municipal Accountant to record the loss in the books of accounts based on the approved Request for Relief from Property Accountability; and

require the Municipal Treasurer, Municipal Engineer and Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator to process the insurance for all insurable properties of the municipality.

 require the Inventory Committee to conduct a physical inventory of the inventory items as of June 30, 2022 submit the RPCI immediately to the Accounting Office for reconciliation.


require the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to reconcile their respective records on these accounts and effect corrections to bring the accounts in order.

1.22   We recommended that the Municipal Mayor should:

c.	require the Municipal Accountant to make the necessary adjustments in the books to: (i) correct all erroneous classification noted, (ii) reconcile her records with the RPCPPE, (iii) reclassify unserviceable property to Other Asset Account; (iv) drop the unserviceable properties from the books after their disposal; and (v) observe the applicable accounting procedures prescribed in COA Circular No. 2020-006 pertaining to recognition/ derecognition of PPE items in the books of account.
1.23   We also recommend that the Accounting Office and Property Unit should undertake collaborative procedures on keeping of records under Items 6.3.1 and 6.3.2 and disposition procedures for non-existing/missing PPEs without available record of accountability under Item 7of COA Circular No. 2020-006 to ensure that all PPEs included in the RPCPPE are duly recorded in their respective records and that the Property Cards maintained by the Property Unit and the PPELCs maintained by the Accounting Unit are reconciled.

a.	Municipal Treasurer to locate the supporting documents of these payables and submit to the Municipal Accountant for examination.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor requires the:
a.	GSO/Property Officer, upon delivery and on the basis of AIR, to record all inventory items procured in separate Stock Cards and furnish the Municipal Accountant copies of the AIR, POs and related documents for recording.  Also, to record issuances of inventory items in the Stock Cards, on the basis of RIS, prepare monthly RSMI consolidating the RIS, and submit copies of the RSMI and RIS to the Municipal Accountant, for recording;
b.	Municipal Accountant to record the purchases/ receipt of deliveries in the books and in the Supply Ledger Cards, on the basis of the copies of AIR, POs and other related documents furnished by the GSO/Property Officer and to record the issuances of inventories as expense and deductions from the Supplies Ledger Card on the basis of the RSMI and RIS submitted by the GSO/Property Officer.
c.	GSO/Property Officer, the Municipal Accountant and the Department Supply Custodian/Distribution Unit to coordinate the conduct of physical count of inventories at the end of the year, to establish the unused inventory balance, to be restored by the Municipal Accountant in the books.
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor requires the Inventory Committee to conduct complete physical count of the unissued supplies, materials and other inventories as of December 31, reconcile the result of physical count with property and accounting records, prepare the corresponding RPCI and submit a copy thereof to the Audit Team not later than January 31 of the ensuing year.

4.15	We recommended that the Municipal Mayor should require the:
a.	Municipal Agriculturist, Municipal Accountant and the Inventory Committee to coordinate and assess the ownership, existence and valuation of Biological Asset for the purpose of establishing its correct account balance; and
b.	Municipal Accountant to prepare the adjusting journal entry based on the result of assessment and furnish the Audit Team copies of the Journal Entry Vouchers, to be supported with the Report on Physical Count of Biological Assets and the project guidelines for animal dispersal and reforestation projects, for further evaluation.

We recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) Chairperson should submit justification why the posting requirement was not complied with and the income/business tax returns and OSS were not attached to the voucher.
Likewise, the Municipal Accountant should submit justification why the voucher was processed and endorsed for payment despite the lack of income/business tax returns, OSS.
Moreover, we recommended that the Municipal Treasurer should require the Supplier to issue Sales Invoices/Official Receipt covering the total amount of materials purchased and not to release payment in the absence thereof.
Moving forward, we recommended that management should comply with pertinent documentary requirements before payment is made to avoid audit suspensions.

We recommended that:
the Municipal Treasurer should require suppliers to issue Sales Invoices or Official Receipts, whichever is appropriate, in complete details and correct amount; and
the Municipal Accountant should submit to the Audit Team the lacking Sales Invoices, otherwise, said transactions shall be suspended in audit and may subsequently be disallowed.
6.13 	As of this writing, the Municipal Treasurer already submitted to the Audit Team the lacking Sales Invoices. Prospectively, the MTO staff are already instructed to ask for the supplier’s accurately filled-up Invoices/Official Receipts prior to release of the check.

We recommended that Municipal Mayor should see to it that the 20% DF shall be utilized optimally for priority PPAs and fast-track the implementation and completion of projects to maximize the realization of their intended purposes in accordance with the provisions of DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2017-1.
We also recommended that the Inspection and Acceptance Committee send notice of delivery to the Audit Team within 24 hours after the acceptance of delivered items has been made.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor should issue an order to the contractor to undertake repair of the defects/deficiencies in the work items of the project. Otherwise, the municipality shall undertake such repair works subject to reimbursement from the contractor of the expenses incurred.
We also recommended that the Municipal Engineer should submit, for the Audit Team’s evaluation, justification on the difference between the quantities of materials used and ordered in the project, else, the amount of ₱476,177.64shall be disallowed in audit.
Further, we recommended that the Inspectorate Team should exercise diligence in the inspection of completed projects to make sure that all materials and procedures prescribed conformed with the Program of Work and Detailed Estimates.
Finally, we recommended that management should ensure that the projects implemented by the municipality are in accordance with the approved program of works and detailed estimates.

Municipal Accountant to carefully examine the completeness of supporting documents in every transaction of the municipality.

observations.managementAction: We recommended that the Local School Board, through the Municipal Mayor, approve the use of the Special Education Fund only for the improvement of school facilities in order to maximize the utilization of SEF for purposes required in DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1, Series of 2017.

observations.recommendation: Management failed to render reports pertaining to the operations as well as  financial position and conditions of its Barangay Bagsakan Project as required under Article II (B), Section 7 of the Memo-randum of Agreement (MOA) between the LGU Hindang and Depart-ment of Agriculture- Regional Field Unit (DA-RFU) No. 8, thus, there is no basis for monitoring and evaluation.
observations.managementAction: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the local agency officials concerned to strictly comply with the requirements stated in sections 119, 120 and 124, Volume I, of the Local Government New Government Accounting System Manual.

observations.recommendation:
12. We recommended that the Municipal Engineer assess the remaining  useful
life of infrastructure assets acquired by the LGU in previous years and
submit a report thereon to the Municipal Accountant. We also  recommend
that the Municipal Accountant, upon receipt  of  the  report  from  the
Municipal Engineer, compute the depreciation expense on the assets  and
every year thereafter, in order to reflect its proper carrying value.

6. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant record  the  purchases  of
construction materials as Inventory upon receipt.   We  also  recommend
that the designated Supply Officer issue an  approved  Requisition  and
Issue Slip (RIS) upon issuance and require a  separate  RIS  from  end-
users for their issuances which should be summarized monthly to support
the Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued (SSMI) to be submitted  to
the Municipal Accountant.

9. We recommended that the Municipal  Mayor  and  the  Municipal  Engineer
implement all development projects to ensure the optimal utilization of
the 20% DF.  If not viable, we recommend  that  the  Local  Development
Council include in the AIP only the topmost priority projects that  can
be implemented during the year.

9. We recommended that Municipal Engineer expedite the  implementation  of
projects to attain its objective.

10. We also recommended that  the  Municipal  Mayor  communicate  with  the
official concerned at DOST RO8 on the limitations  encountered  in  the
implementation of its project, otherwise, return the fund to the Bureau
of Treasury.

5.8   We recommended that the Municipal DRRMO implement during the year  all
programs and projects identified by the MDRRM  Council  to  ensure  the
LGUs readiness to respond in times of calamity.

11. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and the Council design measures
and adopt strategies to remedy the high expenditures in the  operations
of Hindang Zipline and Vermi Culture Production. Review the  plans  and
targets in the operations of these activities in order to  address  the
areas where the municipality can generate additional income.

12. We also recommended that the Municipal Treasurer pay the taxes  due  to
the national government for the income derived from  the  six  economic
enterprises  of  the  Municipality  which  is  proprietary  in  nature.
Otherwise, the Municipality must present documents to  prove  that  the
purpose  of  its  creation,  the  manner  of  its  operation,  intended
recipients of its activities and the disposition of revenues are purely
governmental in nature.

8.5   We recommended that the LGU provide a top cover of  its  RCA;  operate
the  Composter  and  include  in  the  Annual  Procurement   Plan   the
procurement of a densifier and brick-maker.

The Property, Plant and Equipment (PPE) balance with acquisition cost of P161,086,474.40 as of December 31, 2017 does not reconcile with the balance per Report of Physical Count of PPE amounting P94,426,998.27 or a difference of P63,659,476.13, thus, existence, accuracy and reliability of the balances of PPE accounts in the financial statements cannot be ascertained.
observations.managementAction:
We recommended that Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer’s Office reconcile, at least once a month, the inventory reports and accounting records to identify causes of the discrepancy so that necessary adjustment in the books could be made. The Property Officer should maintain and update the property cards to facilitate reconciliation with the Property, Plant and Equipment Ledger Cards maintained by the Municipal Accountant.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant draw journal entries to account for the unserviceable items once disposal of the same has been approved.

Further, we recommended that the accountable officer apply for the destruction of the valueless or unsalable properties upon inspection by the head of the agency or his duly authorized representative in the presence of the auditor concerned. And if found to be valuable, the unserviceable properties may be sold at public auction to the highest bidder under the supervision of the proper committee on award or similar body in the presence of the auditor concerned or other duly authorized representative of the Commission.
observations.reason: Partially Implemented/  Reconciliation is not yet finished.

observations.recommendation: The Municipal Treasurer collected P3,235,462.75, or 24% only, of the total P13,758,164.22 Real Property/ Special Education Tax Receivables as of December 31, 2017, no action for the collection of the tax, whether administrative or judicial was instituted and no report on the delinquent accounts were submitted to the Sangguniang Bayan, contrary to Section 256 and 269 of Republic Act No. 7160,thus the municipality was deprived of the funds for its development projects while funds needed for the operations and maintenance of public schools were not augmented.
observations.managementAction:
We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer:

Develop an effective Tax Collection Campaign program to intensify and maximize collection of Real Property/ Special Education Tax Receivables.
Deputize the Barangay Treasurer, in addition to the collectors of the Municipality, to collect all taxes on real property located in the Barangay, provided that the Barangay Treasurer is properly bonded for the purpose.

Issue a notice of delinquency to each concerned property owner and post the same at the main entrance of the Municipal Hall and in a publicly accessible and conspicuous place in each Barangay of the Local Government Unit of Hindang.

Prepare a certified list of all real property tax delinquencies which remained uncollected or unpaid for at least one (1) year, and a statement of the reason or reasons for such non-collection or non-payment, and submit the same to the Sanggunian concerned on or before December thirty-first (31st) of the year immediately succeeding the year in which the delinquencies were incurred, with a request for assistance in the enforcement of the remedies for collection either by administrative action thru levy on real property or by judicial action.

Issue a warrant of levy to the delinquent owner of the real property or person having legal interest therein. At the same time, written notice of the levy with the attached warrant shall be mailed to or served upon the assessor and the Registrar of Deeds of the province or municipality where the property is located.

File a civil action in any court of competent jurisdiction within the prescribed period to collect the real property tax.

observations.recommendation: Receivables totaling to P1,089,142.35 which remained long outstanding or dormant in the books for ten (10) years were not acted upon to facilitate the proper write-off thereof, resulting in the overstatement of agency assets due to the inclusion of accounts with doubtful existence.
observations.managementAction:
We recommended for the Municipal Mayor to file a request for authority to write-off dormant receivable accounts from COA.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant provide the schedule of the said dormant accounts certified by him/her and approved by the Head of the government entity, complete with certified relevant documents validating the existence of the conditions as applicable in accordance with the procedures in the write-off of dormant accounts as stated in COA Circular 2016-005.

observations.recommendation: The Cash In Bank account is overstated by at least P321,200.09 due to unrecorded items for adjustment in the LGU books, contrary to Sections 3.1, 3.2 and 3.3 of COA Circular No. 96-022, thus defeating the purpose for its preparation and affecting the accuracy of the reported cash balance in the financial statements
observations.managementAction: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant investigate the differences and if no longer traceable, prepare journal entries to put the account in order. Henceforth, see to it that all valid reconciling items are recorded immediately to effect the adjustments and corrections in the General Ledger.

observations.recommendation: The validity, accuracy and existence of the reported Inventory accounts in the financial statements for calendar year 2017 totaling P2,862,759.41 cannot be fully relied upon due to erroneous accounting treatment, absence of inventory reports and non-maintenance of perpetual inventory records contrary to Section 111 and 122 of P.D. 1445.
observations.managementAction:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Treasurer or the designated Property Officer, to conduct physical count of Inventories on stock every six (6) months and submit the Report of Physical Count of Inventories to the Auditor not later than July 31 and January 31 of each year for the first and second semesters, respectively, and to coordinate with the Accounting Office for the reconciliation of the inventory report with the Stock Cards and Supplies Ledger Cards.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant refrain from recognizing an asset when ownership is not yet vested to the LGU.

observations.recommendation: Fund Transfers to KALAHI CIDSS and other administrative expenses totaling P2,869,141.76 which do not partake the nature of investment and capital expenditure, were paid out of the 20% Development Fund, contrary to DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2017-1, thereby reducing the funds intended for development projects that can  provide socio-economic and environmental benefits to the citizens of the municipality.
observations.managementAction:
We recommended that the Municipal Budget Officer and the Municipal Accountant stops appropriating and utilizing the 20% Development Fund for administrative expenses to avoid disallowances in audit.

We also recommended that the Local Chief Executive approve transactions for the use of the 20% Development Fund only if it is strictly in accordance with DILG-DBM Joint Circular No. 2017-1.

observations.recommendation: Project implementation rate in terms of quantity of projects completed was 35% only of the 20 development projects programmed in CY 2017 and with 1 partially completed and 12 unimplemented projects, the desired socio-economic, developmental, and environmental benefits due to the constituents as prescribed by the DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular 2017-1 were not fully attained
observations.managementAction: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive ensure that projects identified in the AIP are fully implemented during the year to maximize the utilization of the 20% DF for the implementation of development projects in accordance with the mandate of the LGC and DILG DBM JMC 2017-1.

observations.recommendation: The Local School Board did not appropriate in its budget funds intended for the Early Childhood Care Development (ECCD) program, contrary to pertinent provisions of Republic Act No. 10410 and DEPED-DBM-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 1 s, 2017, thus, priority programs and activities for children 4 years and below was not undertaken.
observations.managementAction: We recommended that the Local School Board Council incorporate in its budget programs intended for Early Childhood Care Development.

observations.recommendation: Communication and other disaster equipment and supplies of LGU Hindang purchased from its Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management Fund (LDRRMF) were not functional and available, contrary to the purpose for which the items were procured, thus the LGU may not be able to respond quickly to disasters and calamities.
observations.managementAction: We recommended that the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction Management Officer (MDRRMO) safeguard all rescue equipment and supplies and ensure that all are functional and ready at times in order that the LGU will be able to respond quickly to any disaster and normalize the situation as soon as possible.

observations.recommendation: The amount allocated for the Local Council for the Protection of Children (LCPC) of P275,000.00 is deficient by P380,801.00 of the required 1% of the Internal Revenue Allotment (IRA), of which only 66% was utilized, contrary to Section 15 of Republic Act No. 9344 and DILG Memorandum Circular 2012-120, thus, the capacity of the LCPC to effectively implement its programs, projects and activities was not strengthened.
observations.managementAction:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, as Chairman of the Local Council for the Protection of Children, ensure that sufficient amount is appropriated in the Municipal Budget for LCPC activities and programs. The LCPC should maximize the utilization of such amount in accordance with its intended purpose.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Budget Officer to allocate the required amount of 1% of the IRA for the programs, projects and activities for the protection of children in compliance with Section 15 of RA 9344.

observations.recommendation: LGU Hindang has not yet constructed a temporary Residual Containment Area and a Composting Facility as required by the Environment Management Bureau.
observations.managementAction:
We recommended that the LGU expedite the establishment of a Composting Facility and the prescribed Residual Containment Area in order not to disrupt the processing of the garbage.

We also recommended to include in the Annual Investment Program the purchase of densifier and brick-maker.

observations.recommendation: Funds transferred from the National Government Agencies totaling P16.8M were not utilized and the intended nine (9) projects and programs were not implemented due to incomplete pre-requisite documents and inadequate technical personnel in the LGU, contrary to DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2015-45, thus the LGU performance on BuB project implementation may be adversely affected and the intended beneficiaries of the programs/ projects were deprived of its expected benefits.
observations.managementAction: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, as the Chairman of the LPRAT, designate a BuB Focal Person who will coordinate with the different heads of offices to which the program and project are associated, including obtaining copies of duly signed MOAs for the guidance of all concerned. Also, to fast- track the implementation of programs and projects funded by the National Government in accordance with the timeline provided for in the MOA.

observations.recommendation: Construction of the New Hindang Public Market (building A) worth P16.9M with original completion date on November 12, 2016 was already delayed with only 84% accomplishment as of year-end, contrary to the Contract Agreement, while time extensions granted were not adequately supported with valid reasons, contrary to Annex E, IRR, RA 9184, thus depriving the municipality from generating additional revenue that could substantially sustain the LGU’s operational needs.
observations.managementAction: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant deduct the amount of liquidated damages from any amount due to the contractor for any unjustifiable delay in the implementation of the project.

observations.recommendation: The LGU was not fully compliant with the basic environmental regulations on preserving and protecting the environment as required under RA No. 9003 or the Solid Waste Management Act of 2000, thus, the health of its residents and the environment were not protected from harmful effects of improper waste disposal.
observations.managementAction:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:

Provide the necessary assistance in enjoining the barangays to establish their own MRFs and implement waste segregation at source;
Embark on projects and programs to effectively support the LGUs ecological Soled Waste Management goals; and
Strictly comply with all the prescribed procedures and guidelines under RA No. 9003

observations.recommendation: The Special Education Fund (SEF) reflected an unremitted share of collections to provincial school board amounting, to P175,332.87 depriving the targeted recipients of projects of improvement of public school facilities of the expected benefits therefrom.
observations.managementAction: We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer to coordinate with the Municipal Accountant to trace the prior years’ unremitted share of SEF collections. Likewise obtain the necessary supporting documents to support the remittance of the share of SEF collections to the Provincial School Board.

observations.recommendation: The Cash in Bank account balances totaling P53,780,487. 64 could not be ascertained due to the existence of outstanding checks that were already staled for more that two years and other reconciling items which remained untraceable up to the present contrary to Section 97 of PD 1445 and sound internal control, thus, balances reflected in the financial statements were unreliable.
observations.managementAction: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to strictly observed Section 97 of PD 1445. It was further recommended to review and evaluate the reconciling items reflected in the BRS and to prepare the necessary adjusting entries to avoid its repetitive inclusion in subsequent BRS.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal
Treasurer reconcile, at least once a month, the inventory reports and accounting
records to identify the causes of the discrepancy so that necessary adjustment in the books could be made. The Property Officer should maintain and update the property
cards to facilitate reconciliation with the Property, Plant and Equipment Ledger
Cards maintained by the Municipal Accountant.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant draw journal entries to
reclassify the items costing below P15,000.00 as well as the dropping of the accounts
for the unserviceable items once disposal of the same has been approved in
accordance with regulations on property disposal.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer:

a. Develop an effective Tax Collection Campaign program to intensify and
maximize collection of Real Property/Special Education Tax Receivables.

b. Deputize the Barangay Treasurer, in addition to the collectors of the
Municipality, to collect all taxes on real property located in the Barangay,
provided that the Barangay Treasurer is properly bonded for the purpose.

c. Issue a notice of delinquency to each concerned property owner and post the same
at the main entrance of the Municipal Hall and in a publicly accessible and
conspicuous place in each Barangay of the Local Government Unit of Hindang.

d. Prepare a certified list of all real property tax delinquencies which remained
uncollected or unpaid for at least one (1) year, and a statement of the reason or
reasons for such non-collection or non-payment, and submit the same to the
Sanggunian concerned on or before December thirty-first (31st) of the year
immediately succeeding the year in which the delinquencies were incurred, with
a request for assistance in the enforcement of the remedies for collection either by
administrative action thru levy on real property or by judicial action.

e. Issue a warrant of levy to the delinquent owner of the real property or person
having legal interest therein. At the same time, written notice of the levy with the
attached warrant shall be mailed to or served upon the assessor and the Registrar
of Deeds of the municipality where the property is located.

f. File a civil action in any court of competent jurisdiction within the prescribed
period to collect the real property tax.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare and certify the list
of dormant accounts receivables and obtain the complete relevant documents to
validate its eligibility for write-off. Submit the list with the proof of dormancy to the
Municipal Mayor for his approval.

We also recommended for the Municipal Mayor file a request for authority to
write-off dormant receivable accounts with COA based on the documents submitted
by the Municipal Accountant.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant investigate the differences
and if no longer traceable, prepare journal entries to put the account in order.
Henceforth, see to it that all valid reconciling items are recorded immediately to effect
the adjustments and corrections in the General Ledger.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal
Treasurer or the designated Property Officer, to conduct physical count of
Inventories on stock every six (6) months and submit the Report of Physical Count of
Inventories to the Auditor not later than July 31 and January 31 of each year for the
first and second semesters, respectively, and to coordinate with the Accounting Office
for the reconciliation of the inventory report with the Stock Cards and Supplies
Ledger Cards.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant refrain from
recognizing an asset when ownership is not yet vested to the LGU.

We recommended that the Municipal Budget Officer and the Municipal
Accountant stops appropriating and utilizing the 20% Development Fund for
administrative expenses to avoid disallowances in audit.

We also recommended that the Local Chief Executive approve transactions
for the use of the 20% Development Fund only if it is strictly in accordance with
DILG-DBM Joint Circular No. 2017-1.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive ensure that projects
identified in the AIP are fully implemented during the year to maximize the utilization
of the 20% DF for the implementation of development projects in accordance with
the mandate of the LGC and DILG/DBM JMC 2017-1.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the designated Chairman
of the Physical Inventory Committee to prepare an inventory report of all insurable
properties of the Municipality and submit the list to the General Insurance Fund of
the GSIS for proper processing of the insurance coverage of all insurable properties
to ensure that the LGU will be indemnified in case of loss, destruction and or damages.

We also recommended that the Municipal Budget Officer ensure the
availability of appropriations for the cost of insurance coverage as computed by the
GSIS.

We recommended that the Local School Board, through the Municipal Mayor,
approve the use of the Special Education Fund only for the improvement of school
facilities in order to maximize the utilization of SEF for purposes required in DepEd-
DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1, Series of 2017.

We recommended that the Local School Board Council incorporate in its
budget programs and activities intended for Early Childhood Care Development.

We recommended that the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction Management
Officer (MDRRMO) safeguard all rescue equipment and supplies and ensure that all
are functional and ready at all times in order that the LGU will be able to respond
quickly to any disaster and normalize the situation as soon as possible.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, as Chairman of the Local
Council for the Protection of Children, ensure that sufficient amount is appropriated
in the Municipal Budget for LCPC activities and programs. The LCPC should
maximize the utilization of such amount in accordance with its intended purpose.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Budget
Officer to allocate the required amount of 1% of the IRA for the programs, projects
and activities for the protection of children in compliance with Section 15 of RA 9344.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, through the Municipal SWM
Officer, ensure that the LGU comply strictly with the Solid Waste Management Act
by providing a space for the materials in order to continue the operation of the plastic
shredder. Also, include the procurement of a densifier and brick-maker in the
Annual Procurement Plan in order to ensure that appropriate funds are set aside for
such purposes.

We recommend  that  the  Municipal  Mayor  require  the  Committee  on
Physical Inventory to complete the annual physical count of LGU  assets  and
submit the updated RPCPPE not later than January 31 of each year.   Further,
require the Municipal Accountant and the designated  Property  Custodian  to
maintain ledger cards and property cards, respectively, and reconcile  their
records regularly as basis in the reconciliation of the RPCPPE.

      We reiterate our recommendation that the Disposal  Committee  complete
the  documentary  requirements  for  the  disposal  of   the   unserviceable
properties and submit the request for authority  to  the  COA.   Thereafter,
furnish the Municipal Accountant with a copy of the approval  as  basis  for
the dropping of the PPE accounts in the books of the LGU.

We recommend that the Municipal Engineer institute  remedial  measures
on the contractor to accelerate the work  on  the  project  and  demand  its
immediate completion.  Also, monitor closely the implementation of  projects
to ensure that the interests of the LGU are safeguarded.

      We recommend that  the  Municipal  Accountant  deduct  the  amount  of
liquidated  damages  from  any  amount  due  to  the  contractor   for   any
unjustifiable delay in the implementation of the project.

on the contractor to accelerate the work  on  the  project  and  demand  its
immediate completion.  Also, monitor closely the implementation of  projects
to ensure that the interests of the LGU are safeguarded.

      We recommend that  the  Municipal  Accountant  deduct  the  amount  of
liquidated  damages  from  any  amount  due  to  the  contractor   for   any
unjustifiable delay in the implementation of the project.

We recommend that the Local Chief  Executive  consider  implement  all
projects included in the Annual Investment Plan  of  the  LGU  in  order  to
utilize fully the 20% development  fund  in  accordance  with  its  intended
purposes.

      Also, we recommend that the Municipal Engineer perform  his  functions
thoroughly and ensure that the proposed  annual  projects  of  the  LGU  are
fully implemented through proper scheduling and close supervision.

We recommend that the MDRRM Council, through the  MDRRMO,  include  in
the annual MDRRMF Investment Plan programs, projects and  activities  to  be
funded from the unexpended MDRRM funds which was accumulated  for  the  past
five years.   Also,  we  recommend  that  the  MDRRMO  initiate  and  device
strategies  to  speed  up  implementation  of  projects  for  disaster  risk
reduction and management activities to ensure readiness of the LGU  in  case
of calamity.

We recommend that the Municipal School Board appropriate  and  utilize
the SEF budget for the improvement of school facilities in  accordance  with
the purpose for which it was created.  Also, stop payments  of  expenditures
which are not in consonance with the guidelines.

We recommend that the Local Chief Executive: (a) provide the necessary
assistance in enjoining the  barangays  to  establish  their  own  MRFs  and
implement waste segregation  at  source;  (b)  implement  strictly  the  “no
segregation, no collection” policy; (c) embark on projects and  programs  to
effectively support the LGUs ecological solid waste  management  goals;  and
d) strictly comply with all the prescribed procedures and guidelines  under
RA No. 9003.
